root_text.an4.198:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo attantapo hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:1.4: Idha pana, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo parantapo hoti paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:1.5: Idha pana, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo attantapo ca hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto, parantapo ca paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:1.6: Idha pana, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo nevattantapo hoti nāttaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto na parantapo na paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:1.7: So neva attantapo na parantapo diṭṭheva dhamme nicchāto nibbuto sītībhūto sukhappaṭisaṁvedī brahmabhūtena attanā viharati.
root_text.an4.198:2.1: Kathañca, bhikkhave, puggalo attantapo hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto?
root_text.an4.198:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco acelako hoti muttācāro hatthāpalekhano naehibhaddantiko natiṭṭhabhaddantiko nābhihaṭaṁ na uddissakataṁ na nimantanaṁ sādiyati.
root_text.an4.198:2.3: So na kumbhimukhā paṭiggaṇhāti, na kaḷopimukhā paṭiggaṇhāti, na eḷakamantaraṁ na daṇḍamantaraṁ na musalamantaraṁ na dvinnaṁ bhuñjamānānaṁ na gabbhiniyā na pāyamānāya na purisantaragatāya na saṅkittīsu na yattha sā upaṭṭhito hoti na yattha makkhikā saṇḍasaṇḍacārinī na macchaṁ na maṁsaṁ na suraṁ na merayaṁ na thusodakaṁ pivati.
root_text.an4.198:2.4: So ekāgāriko vā hoti ekālopiko dvāgāriko vā hoti dvālopiko …pe… sattāgāriko vā hoti sattālopiko;
root_text.an4.198:2.5: ekissāpi dattiyā yāpeti dvīhipi dattīhi yāpeti …pe… sattahipi dattīhi yāpeti;
root_text.an4.198:2.6: ekāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti dvāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti …pe… sattāhikampi āhāraṁ āhāreti. Iti evarūpaṁ aḍḍhamāsikampi pariyāyabhattabhojanānuyogamanuyutto viharati.
root_text.an4.198:3.1: So sākabhakkhopi hoti sāmākabhakkhopi hoti nīvārabhakkhopi hoti daddulabhakkhopi hoti haṭabhakkhopi hoti kaṇabhakkhopi hoti ācāmabhakkhopi hoti piññākabhakkhopi hoti tiṇabhakkhopi hoti gomayabhakkhopi hoti; vanamūlaphalāhāropi yāpeti pavattaphalabhojī.
root_text.an4.198:4.1: So sāṇānipi dhāreti masāṇānipi dhāreti chavadussānipi dhāreti paṁsukūlānipi dhāreti tirīṭānipi dhāreti ajinampi dhāreti ajinakkhipampi dhāreti kusacīrampi dhāreti vākacīrampi dhāreti phalakacīrampi dhāreti kesakambalampi dhāreti vāḷakambalampi dhāreti ulūkapakkhampi dhāreti;
root_text.an4.198:4.7: Iti evarūpaṁ anekavihitaṁ kāyassa ātāpanaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto viharati.
root_text.an4.198:4.8: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, puggalo attantapo hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:5.1: Kathañca, bhikkhave, puggalo parantapo hoti paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto?
root_text.an4.198:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo orabbhiko hoti sūkariko sākuṇiko māgaviko luddo macchaghātako coro coraghātako goghātako bandhanāgāriko, ye vā panaññepi keci kurūrakammantā.
root_text.an4.198:5.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, puggalo parantapo hoti paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:6.1: Kathañca, bhikkhave, puggalo attantapo ca hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto parantapo ca paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto?
root_text.an4.198:6.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco puggalo rājā vā hoti khattiyo muddhāvasitto, brāhmaṇo vā hoti mahāsālo.
root_text.an4.198:6.3: So puratthimena nagarassa navaṁ santhāgāraṁ kārāpetvā kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kharājinaṁ nivāsetvā sappitelena kāyaṁ abbhañjitvā magavisāṇena piṭṭhiṁ kaṇḍuvamāno navaṁ santhāgāraṁ pavisati, saddhiṁ mahesiyā brāhmaṇena ca purohitena.
root_text.an4.198:6.4: So tattha anantarahitāya bhūmiyā haritupalittāya seyyaṁ kappeti.
root_text.an4.198:6.5: Ekissāya gāviyā sarūpavacchāya yaṁ ekasmiṁ thane khīraṁ hoti tena rājā yāpeti; yaṁ dutiyasmiṁ thane khīraṁ hoti tena mahesī yāpeti; yaṁ tatiyasmiṁ thane khīraṁ hoti tena brāhmaṇo purohito yāpeti; yaṁ catutthasmiṁ thane khīraṁ hoti tena aggiṁ juhati; avasesena vacchako yāpeti.
root_text.an4.198:6.7: ‘ettakā usabhā haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā vacchatarā haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā vacchatariyo haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā ajā haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā urabbhā haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā assā haññantu yaññatthāya, ettakā rukkhā chijjantu yūpatthāya, ettakā dabbhā lūyantu barihisatthāyā’ti.
root_text.an4.198:6.8: Yepissa te honti dāsāti vā pessāti vā kammakarāti vā tepi daṇḍatajjitā bhayatajjitā assumukhā rudamānā parikammāni karonti.
root_text.an4.198:6.9: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, puggalo attantapo ca hoti attaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto parantapo ca paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:7.1: Kathañca, bhikkhave, puggalo nevattantapo hoti nāttaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto na parantapo na paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto?
root_text.an4.198:7.2: So anattantapo aparantapo diṭṭheva dhamme nicchāto nibbuto sītībhūto sukhappaṭisaṁvedī brahmabhūtena attanā viharati.
root_text.an4.198:7.3: Idha, bhikkhave, tathāgato loke uppajjati arahaṁ sammāsambuddho vijjācaraṇasampanno sugato lokavidū anuttaro purisadammasārathi satthā devamanussānaṁ buddho bhagavā.
root_text.an4.198:7.4: So imaṁ lokaṁ sadevakaṁ samārakaṁ sabrahmakaṁ sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiṁ pajaṁ sadevamanussaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā pavedeti.
root_text.an4.198:7.5: So dhammaṁ deseti ādikalyāṇaṁ majjhekalyāṇaṁ pariyosānakalyāṇaṁ sātthaṁ sabyañjanaṁ, kevalaparipuṇṇaṁ parisuddhaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pakāseti.
root_text.an4.198:7.6: Taṁ dhammaṁ suṇāti gahapati vā gahapatiputto vā aññatarasmiṁ vā kule paccājāto.
root_text.an4.198:7.7: So taṁ dhammaṁ sutvā tathāgate saddhaṁ paṭilabhati.
root_text.an4.198:7.8: So tena saddhāpaṭilābhena samannāgato iti paṭisañcikkhati:
root_text.an4.198:7.10: nayidaṁ sukaraṁ agāraṁ ajjhāvasatā ekantaparipuṇṇaṁ ekantaparisuddhaṁ saṅkhalikhitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ carituṁ;
root_text.an4.198:7.11: yannūnāhaṁ kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajeyyan’ti.
root_text.an4.198:7.12: So aparena samayena appaṁ vā bhogakkhandhaṁ pahāya, mahantaṁ vā bhogakkhandhaṁ pahāya, appaṁ vā ñātiparivaṭṭaṁ pahāya, mahantaṁ vā ñātiparivaṭṭaṁ pahāya, kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajati.
root_text.an4.198:8.1: So evaṁ pabbajito samāno bhikkhūnaṁ sikkhāsājīvasamāpanno pāṇātipātaṁ pahāya pāṇātipātā paṭivirato hoti nihitadaṇḍo nihitasattho lajjī dayāpanno, sabbapāṇabhūtahitānukampī viharati.
root_text.an4.198:8.2: Adinnādānaṁ pahāya adinnādānā paṭivirato hoti dinnādāyī dinnapāṭikaṅkhī, athenena sucibhūtena attanā viharati.
root_text.an4.198:8.3: Abrahmacariyaṁ pahāya brahmacārī hoti ārācārī virato asaddhammā gāmadhammā.
root_text.an4.198:8.4: Musāvādaṁ pahāya musāvādā paṭivirato hoti saccavādī saccasandho theto paccayiko avisaṁvādako lokassa.
root_text.an4.198:8.5: Pisuṇaṁ vācaṁ pahāya pisuṇāya vācāya paṭivirato hoti, na ito sutvā amutra akkhātā imesaṁ bhedāya, na amutra vā sutvā imesaṁ akkhātā amūsaṁ bhedāya; iti bhinnānaṁ vā sandhātā, sahitānaṁ vā anuppadātā, samaggārāmo samaggarato samagganandī samaggakaraṇiṁ vācaṁ bhāsitā hoti.
root_text.an4.198:8.6: Pharusaṁ vācaṁ pahāya pharusāya vācāya paṭivirato hoti; yā sā vācā nelā kaṇṇasukhā pemanīyā hadayaṅgamā porī bahujanakantā bahujanamanāpā tathārūpiṁ vācaṁ bhāsitā hoti.
root_text.an4.198:8.7: Samphappalāpaṁ pahāya samphappalāpā paṭivirato hoti kālavādī bhūtavādī atthavādī dhammavādī vinayavādī; nidhānavatiṁ vācaṁ bhāsitā hoti kālena sāpadesaṁ pariyantavatiṁ atthasaṁhitaṁ.
root_text.an4.198:10.1: So santuṭṭho hoti kāyaparihārikena cīvarena kucchiparihārikena piṇḍapātena. So yena yeneva pakkamati samādāyeva pakkamati.
root_text.an4.198:10.2: Seyyathāpi nāma pakkhī sakuṇo yena yeneva ḍeti, sapattabhārova ḍeti;
root_text.an4.198:10.3: evamevaṁ bhikkhu santuṭṭho hoti kāyaparihārikena cīvarena kucchiparihārikena piṇḍapātena. So yena yeneva pakkamati, samādāyeva pakkamati.
root_text.an4.198:10.4: So iminā ariyena sīlakkhandhena samannāgato ajjhattaṁ anavajjasukhaṁ paṭisaṁvedeti.
root_text.an4.198:11.1: So cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā na nimittaggāhī hoti nānubyañjanaggāhī.
root_text.an4.198:11.2: Yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ cakkhundriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjati; rakkhati cakkhundriyaṁ; cakkhundriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjati.
root_text.an4.198:11.7: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya na nimittaggāhī hoti nānubyañjanaggāhī.
root_text.an4.198:11.8: Yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ manindriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjati; rakkhati manindriyaṁ; manindriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjati.
root_text.an4.198:11.9: So iminā ariyena indriyasaṁvarena samannāgato ajjhattaṁ abyāsekasukhaṁ paṭisaṁvedeti.
root_text.an4.198:12.1: So abhikkante paṭikkante sampajānakārī hoti, ālokite vilokite sampajānakārī hoti, samiñjite pasārite sampajānakārī hoti, saṅghāṭipattacīvaradhāraṇe sampajānakārī hoti, asite pīte khāyite sāyite sampajānakārī hoti, uccārapassāvakamme sampajānakārī hoti, gate ṭhite nisinne sutte jāgarite bhāsite tuṇhībhāve sampajānakārī hoti.
root_text.an4.198:13.1: So iminā ca ariyena sīlakkhandhena samannāgato, imāya ca ariyāya santuṭṭhiyā samannāgato, iminā ca ariyena indriyasaṁvarena samannāgato, iminā ca ariyena satisampajaññena samannāgato
root_text.an4.198:13.2: vivittaṁ senāsanaṁ bhajati araññaṁ rukkhamūlaṁ pabbataṁ kandaraṁ giriguhaṁ susānaṁ vanappatthaṁ abbhokāsaṁ palālapuñjaṁ.
root_text.an4.198:13.3: So pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkanto nisīdati pallaṅkaṁ ābhujitvā ujuṁ kāyaṁ paṇidhāya parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā.
root_text.an4.198:13.4: So abhijjhaṁ loke pahāya vigatābhijjhena cetasā viharati, abhijjhāya cittaṁ parisodheti.
root_text.an4.198:13.5: Byāpādapadosaṁ pahāya abyāpannacitto viharati sabbapāṇabhūtahitānukampī, byāpādapadosā cittaṁ parisodheti.
root_text.an4.198:13.6: Thinamiddhaṁ pahāya vigatathinamiddho viharati ālokasaññī sato sampajāno, thinamiddhā cittaṁ parisodheti.
root_text.an4.198:13.7: Uddhaccakukkuccaṁ pahāya anuddhato viharati ajjhattaṁ vūpasantacitto, uddhaccakukkuccā cittaṁ parisodheti.
root_text.an4.198:13.8: Vicikicchaṁ pahāya tiṇṇavicikiccho viharati akathaṅkathī kusalesu dhammesu, vicikicchāya cittaṁ parisodheti.
root_text.an4.198:13.9: So ime pañca nīvaraṇe pahāya cetaso upakkilese paññāya dubbalīkaraṇe
root_text.an4.198:13.10: vivicceva kāmehi …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an4.198:14.1: So evaṁ samāhite citte parisuddhe pariyodāte anaṅgaṇe vigatūpakkilese mudubhūte kammaniye ṭhite āneñjappatte pubbenivāsānussatiñāṇāya …pe…
root_text.an4.198:14.4: So ‘idaṁ dukkhan’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ dukkhasamudayo’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ dukkhanirodho’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti.
root_text.an4.198:14.5: ‘Ime āsavā’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ āsavasamudayo’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ āsavanirodho’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ‘ayaṁ āsavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti.
root_text.an4.198:15.1: Tassa evaṁ jānato evaṁ passato kāmāsavāpi cittaṁ vimuccati, bhavāsavāpi cittaṁ vimuccati, avijjāsavāpi cittaṁ vimuccati;
root_text.an4.198:15.3: ‘Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā’ti pajānāti.
root_text.an4.198:15.4: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, puggalo nevattantapo hoti nāttaparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto na parantapo na paraparitāpanānuyogamanuyutto.
root_text.an4.198:15.5: So na attantapo na parantapo diṭṭheva dhamme nicchāto nibbuto sītībhūto sukhappaṭisaṁvedī brahmabhūtena attanā viharati.
root_text.an4.198:15.6: Ime kho, bhikkhave, cattāro puggalā santo saṁvijjamānā lokasmin”ti.

translation_text.an4.198:1.1: “Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world.
translation_text.an4.198:1.3: One individual mortifies themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves.
translation_text.an4.198:1.4: One individual mortifies others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others.
translation_text.an4.198:1.5: One individual mortifies themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others.
translation_text.an4.198:1.6: One individual neither mortifies themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others.
translation_text.an4.198:1.7: They live without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine.
translation_text.an4.198:2.1: And how does one individual mortify themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves?
translation_text.an4.198:2.2: It’s when someone goes naked, ignoring conventions. They lick their hands, and don’t come or wait when called. They don’t consent to food brought to them, or food prepared on their behalf, or an invitation for a meal.
translation_text.an4.198:2.3: They don’t receive anything from a pot or bowl; or from someone who keeps sheep, or who has a weapon or a shovel in their home; or where a couple is eating; or where there is a woman who is pregnant, breastfeeding, or who lives with a man; or where there’s a hound waiting or flies buzzing. They accept no fish or meat or beer or wine, and drink no fermented gruel.
translation_text.an4.198:2.4: They go to just one house for alms, taking just one mouthful, or two houses and two mouthfuls, up to seven houses and seven mouthfuls.
translation_text.an4.198:2.5: They feed on one saucer a day, two saucers a day, up to seven saucers a day.
translation_text.an4.198:2.6: They eat once a day, once every second day, up to once a week, and so on, even up to once a fortnight. They live pursuing the practice of eating food at set intervals.
translation_text.an4.198:3.1: They eat herbs, millet, wild rice, poor rice, water lettuce, rice bran, scum from boiling rice, sesame flour, grass, or cow dung. They survive on forest roots and fruits, or eating fallen fruit.
translation_text.an4.198:4.1: They wear robes of sunn hemp, mixed hemp, corpse-wrapping cloth, rags, lodh tree bark, antelope hide (whole or in strips), kusa grass, bark, wood-chips, human hair, horse-tail hair, or owls’ wings.
translation_text.an4.198:4.2: They tear out hair and beard, pursuing this practice.
translation_text.an4.198:4.3: They constantly stand, turning down seats.
translation_text.an4.198:4.4: They squat, committed to the endeavor of squatting.
translation_text.an4.198:4.5: They lie on a mat of thorns, making a mat of thorns their bed.
translation_text.an4.198:4.6: They’re devoted to ritual bathing three times a day, including at dusk.
translation_text.an4.198:4.7: And so they live pursuing these various ways of mortifying and tormenting the body.
translation_text.an4.198:4.8: That’s how one individual mortifies themselves, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves.
translation_text.an4.198:5.1: And how does one individual mortify others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others?
translation_text.an4.198:5.2: It’s when an individual is a slaughterer of sheep, pigs, poultry, or deer, a hunter or fisher, a bandit, an executioner, a butcher of cattle, a jailer, or has some other cruel livelihood.
translation_text.an4.198:5.3: That’s how one individual mortifies others, pursuing the practice of mortifying others.
translation_text.an4.198:6.1: And how does one individual mortify themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others?
translation_text.an4.198:6.2: It’s when an individual is an anointed aristocratic king or a well-to-do brahmin.
translation_text.an4.198:6.3: He has a new ceremonial hall built to the east of the citadel. He shaves off his hair and beard, dresses in a rough antelope hide, and smears his body with ghee and oil. Scratching his back with antlers, he enters the hall with his chief queen and the brahmin high priest.
translation_text.an4.198:6.4: There he lies on the bare ground strewn with grass.
translation_text.an4.198:6.5: The king feeds on the milk from one teat of a cow that has a calf of the same color. The chief queen feeds on the milk from the second teat. The brahmin high priest feeds on the milk from the third teat. The milk from the fourth teat is served to the sacred flame. The calf feeds on the leftovers.
translation_text.an4.198:6.7: ‘Slaughter this many bulls, bullocks, heifers, goats, rams, and horses for the sacrifice! Fell this many trees and reap this much grass for the sacrificial equipment!’
translation_text.an4.198:6.8: His bondservants, servants, and workers do their jobs under threat of punishment and danger, weeping, with tearful faces.
translation_text.an4.198:6.9: That’s how one individual mortifies themselves and others, pursuing the practice of mortifying themselves and others.
translation_text.an4.198:7.1: And how does one individual neither mortify themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others,
translation_text.an4.198:7.2: living without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine?
translation_text.an4.198:7.3: It’s when a Realized One arises in the world, perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.
translation_text.an4.198:7.4: He has realized with his own insight this world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—and he makes it known to others.
translation_text.an4.198:7.5: He proclaims a teaching that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure.
translation_text.an4.198:7.6: A householder hears that teaching, or a householder’s child, or someone reborn in a good family.
translation_text.an4.198:7.7: They gain faith in the Realized One
translation_text.an4.198:7.9: ‘Life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open.
translation_text.an4.198:7.10: It’s not easy for someone living at home to lead the spiritual life utterly full and pure, like a polished shell.
translation_text.an4.198:7.11: Why don’t I shave off my hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness?’
translation_text.an4.198:7.12: After some time they give up a large or small fortune, and a large or small family circle. They shave off hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness.
translation_text.an4.198:8.1: Once they’ve gone forth, they take up the training and livelihood of the mendicants. They give up killing living creatures, renouncing the rod and the sword. They’re scrupulous and kind, living full of sympathy for all living beings.
translation_text.an4.198:8.2: They give up stealing. They take only what’s given, and expect only what’s given. They keep themselves clean by not thieving.
translation_text.an4.198:8.3: They give up unchastity. They are chaste, set apart, avoiding the vulgar act of sex.
translation_text.an4.198:8.4: They give up lying. They speak the truth and stick to the truth. They’re honest and dependable, and don’t trick the world with their words.
translation_text.an4.198:8.5: They give up divisive speech. They don’t repeat in one place what they heard in another so as to divide people against each other. Instead, they reconcile those who are divided and support those who are united, delighting in harmony, loving harmony, speaking words that promote harmony.
translation_text.an4.198:8.6: They give up harsh speech. They speak in a way that’s mellow, pleasing to the ear, lovely, going to the heart, polite, likable, and agreeable to the people.
translation_text.an4.198:8.7: They give up talking nonsense. Their words are timely, true, and meaningful, in line with the teaching and training. They say things at the right time which are valuable, reasonable, succinct, and beneficial.
translation_text.an4.198:9.1: They refrain from injuring plants and seeds.
translation_text.an4.198:9.2: They eat in one part of the day, abstaining from eating at night and food at the wrong time.
translation_text.an4.198:9.3: They refrain from seeing shows of dancing, singing, and music .
translation_text.an4.198:9.4: They refrain from attiring and adorning themselves with garlands, fragrance, and makeup.
translation_text.an4.198:9.5: They refrain from high and luxurious beds.
translation_text.an4.198:9.6: They refrain from receiving gold and currency,
translation_text.an4.198:9.15: They refrain from running errands and messages;
translation_text.an4.198:9.19: mutilation, murder, abduction, banditry, plunder, and violence.
translation_text.an4.198:10.1: They’re content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things.
translation_text.an4.198:10.2: They’re like a bird: wherever it flies, wings are its only burden.
translation_text.an4.198:10.3: In the same way, a mendicant is content with robes to look after the body and almsfood to look after the belly. Wherever they go, they set out taking only these things.
translation_text.an4.198:10.4: When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, they experience a blameless happiness inside themselves.
translation_text.an4.198:11.1: Seeing a sight with the eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.an4.198:11.2: If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it.
translation_text.an4.198:11.3: Hearing a sound with the ears …
translation_text.an4.198:11.4: Smelling an odor with the nose …
translation_text.an4.198:11.5: Tasting a flavor with the tongue …
translation_text.an4.198:11.6: Feeling a touch with the body …
translation_text.an4.198:11.7: Knowing an idea with the mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.an4.198:11.8: If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it.
translation_text.an4.198:11.9: When they have this noble sense restraint, they experience an unsullied bliss inside themselves.
translation_text.an4.198:12.1: They act with situational awareness when going out and coming back; when looking ahead and aside; when bending and extending the limbs; when bearing the outer robe, bowl and robes; when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; when urinating and defecating; when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
translation_text.an4.198:13.1: When they have this entire spectrum of noble ethics, this noble contentment, this noble sense restraint, and this noble mindfulness and situational awareness,
translation_text.an4.198:13.2: they frequent a secluded lodging—a wilderness, the root of a tree, a hill, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest, the open air, a heap of straw.
translation_text.an4.198:13.3: After the meal, they return from almsround, sit down cross-legged, set their body straight, and establish mindfulness in their presence.
translation_text.an4.198:13.4: Giving up covetousness for the world, they meditate with a heart rid of covetousness, cleansing the mind of covetousness.
translation_text.an4.198:13.5: Giving up ill will and malevolence, they meditate with a mind rid of ill will, full of sympathy for all living beings, cleansing the mind of ill will.
translation_text.an4.198:13.6: Giving up dullness and drowsiness, they meditate with a mind rid of dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, mindful and aware, cleansing the mind of dullness and drowsiness.
translation_text.an4.198:13.7: Giving up restlessness and remorse, they meditate without restlessness, their mind peaceful inside, cleansing the mind of restlessness and remorse.
translation_text.an4.198:13.8: Giving up doubt, they meditate having gone beyond doubt, not undecided about skillful qualities, cleansing the mind of doubt.
translation_text.an4.198:13.9: They give up these five hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom.
translation_text.an4.198:13.10: Then, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, they enter and remain in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption.
translation_text.an4.198:14.1: When their mind has become immersed in samādhi like this—purified, bright, flawless, rid of corruptions, pliable, workable, steady, and imperturbable—they extend it toward recollection of past lives …
translation_text.an4.198:14.2: knowledge of the death and rebirth of sentient beings …
translation_text.an4.198:14.3: knowledge of the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an4.198:14.4: They truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’.
translation_text.an4.198:14.5: They truly understand: ‘These are defilements’ … ‘This is the origin of defilements’ … ‘This is the cessation of defilements’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of defilements’.
translation_text.an4.198:15.1: Knowing and seeing like this, their mind is freed from the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
translation_text.an4.198:15.2: When they’re freed, they know they’re freed.
translation_text.an4.198:15.3: They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’
translation_text.an4.198:15.4: That’s how one individual neither mortifies themselves nor others, pursuing the practice of not mortifying themselves or others,
translation_text.an4.198:15.5: living without wishes in this very life, quenched, cooled, experiencing bliss, with self become divine.
translation_text.an4.198:15.6: These are the four individuals found in the world.”

variant_text.an4.198:6.7: ettakā assā haññantu yaññatthāya → etthantare pāṭho si, sya-all, km, pts1ed potthakesu, barihisatthāyā’ti → parikammatthāyāti (mr)
variant_text.an4.198:13.1: imāya ca ariyāya santuṭṭhiyā samannāgato → etthantare pāṭho si, sya-all, pts1ed potthakesu | samannāgato → samannāgato. so (mr)
